items.title: Writing for Emotional Impact - by Karl Iglesias
items.summary: About the craft of writing a great film screenplay. A bit tedious (with sloppy repetitive writing) but I always love these books for making me see the craft behind movies I know well, and pointing out how to use this craft to make better stories in any medium.

items.title: tweet
items.summary: Fight Club: I liked it the first time years ago, but watching a second time when you already know the end reveal? Elevates it from good to great. So many brilliant details in the screenplay. What else in life is better the second time?

items.title: Cambridge Language Surveys: Chinese - by Jerry Norman
items.summary: 80’s book about Chinese, helped me understand how simplified Chinese writing came about: not as much top-down as bottom-up formalizing of shorthand and cursive that had been in use for centuries.

items.title: Netizen
items.summary: I can relate to the original meaning of this old term that means to be good citizen of the internet.

items.title: tweet
items.summary: I’m not autistic, but my heroes are.

items.title: tweet
items.summary: Since we moved from city to rural land, to a house where the bathroom and kitchen are outside, my boy observed: “This year there are way more full moons.”

items.title: tweet
items.summary: My favorite LLM prompt: “List the top cities in {COUNTRY}, and anthropomorphize each one, listing the good-natured stereotypes and jokes that other people in {COUNTRY} say about people in this city.”

items.title: You Can Just Do Things - by Cate Hall and Sasha Chapin
items.summary: How to be agentic - to take charge and make things happen, no matter how unusual or uncomfortable it may be. Make your world the way you ideally want it to be, instead of normal. Cate Hall embodies this, so it’s great to hear it from someone really living it instead of just writing about it.

items.title: tweet
items.summary: Good book on cultural psychology: Tribal. Great insights and examples of how change happens on the personal, cultural, and institutional level. My notes at https://sive.rs/book/Tribal

items.title: tweet
items.summary: Fascinating book: Nineteen Ways of Looking at Consciousness - https://sive.rs/book/Conscious19 - uses one true story, 19 times, of the laughing brain patient - https://sive.rs/u12 - in the format of Nineteen Ways of Looking at Wang Wei - https://sive.rs/book/WangWei - to explore the subject.

items.title: tweet
items.summary: Great book: Elephant in the Brain: https://sive.rs/book/BrainMotives You have hidden motives and subconscious incentives. Two reasons for doing anything: a good reason and the real reason. Instititutions do this too: wasteful because they’re serving hidden purposes no one acknowledges. Great insights throughout.

items.title: Travelling While Black - by Nanjala Nyabola
items.summary: Thoughts on human rights, race, gender, travel, inequality, tribe, home, visas, poverty, Kenya, Africa, and Bessie Head.

items.title: Tribal - by Michael Morris
items.summary: I love cultural psychology. Great insights here, and examples of how change happens, on the personal level, cultural level, and even institutional level. American, but lived in Hong Kong for years and I appreciated his example of Singapore.

items.title: Nineteen Ways of Looking at Consciousness - by Patrick House
items.summary: Using the story of Anna - the patient who laughed when the surgeon stimulated parts of her brain - the author looks at the subject of consciousness from 19 different angles, writing in slightly different styles. He used the format of another book I have notes on here, titled “Nineteen Ways of Looking at Wang Wei”. Some beautiful poetic writing here.

items.title: Elephant in the Brain - by Kevin Simler and Robin Hanson
items.summary: You have hidden motives and subconscious incentives. Two reasons for doing anything: a good reason and the real reason. Instititutions do this too: wasteful because they’re serving hidden purposes no one acknowledges. Great insights throughout.

items.title: tweet
items.summary: People of India! My OCI + Airtel + HDFC has culminated in a massive achievement and rite of initiation. I have made my first UPI payment. I’m now unstoppable.

items.title: China in Ten Words - by Yu Hua 余华
items.summary: Famous fiction author. I loved the movie from his book “To Live”. Memoir of growing up in China filtered through ten topics. Sometimes it’s cultural insights, but sometimes just his personal stories. Deep insights on “copycat” and “bamboozle”. Note those words are rough English translations of the real Chinese words he’s discussing.

items.title: tweet
items.summary: “Purpose” and “passion” are words we use when we’re not working.

items.title: I was inconsiderate but now I’m everywhere
items.summary: When I was in the music business, there was a record producer who lived in New Jersey, but refused to come into New York City.

items.title: Maintenance of Everything, part one - by Stewart Brand
items.summary: Because I loved his book “How Buildings Learn”, I expected a more philosophical look at maintenance, but it’s mostly a deep dive into a few specific examples of boats, guns, and cars.

items.title: Offline 23 hours a day
items.summary: Last month, I moved into my new home in the woods.

items.title: tweet
items.summary: Got my own ActivityPub server running at https://sive.rs/d though still super basic and testing

items.title: You’re Not Listening - by Kate Murphy
items.summary: Being a great listener when people speak. Deep insights about understanding, connection, helping people express themselves, overcoming assumptions, the ethics of gossip, and more. Specific techniques for the support response, encouraging elaboration, and keeping it balanced. You can’t be ethical without being a good listener. When people say, “I can’t talk…

items.title: You Will Not Stampede Me - by Bryan Caplan
items.summary: Tiny blog posts about non-conformity. I love his writing in general, but these were topics that I’d already read his writing on before, or too out of my realm (like academic life) to be interesting to me.

items.title: What next?
items.summary: I have so much more to say on this subject, but this book is done now because I believe short books are useful.

items.title: Reframing death
items.summary: For the last three years, my boy and I have had a pet mouse.

items.title: You are what you pretend to be
items.summary: Your outside doesn’t need to match your inside.

items.title: Keep tuning and adjusting
items.summary: When I got my first guitar, the nice man at the shop put on new strings and tuned it.

items.title: Take the first step immediately
items.summary: I spent basically my whole life in America, and had no desire to travel or be anywhere else.

items.title: Why your choice is wrong
items.summary: No matter what choice you make, someone will tell you it’s wrong.

items.title: Talk with friends to solidify it
items.summary: After you privately internalize a belief, talk about it with friends.

items.title: Private journal to internalize it
items.summary: Once you find a viewpoint you want to adopt, a great tool to internalize it is a private journal.

items.title: No new instructions for the computer
items.summary: You load the program into the computer, and it begins its calculations.

items.title: From explorer to self-leader
items.summary: Picture the stereotype of an explorer, hundreds of years ago, on an expedition to uncharted lands.

items.title: How to decide and make the best choice
items.summary: You can do anything.

items.title: An awesome collection of great questions
items.summary: This is where I would share powerful questions that you can answer for big insights and change.

items.title: Five tiny tales of reframing
items.summary: On the Olympic podium stood the winners of the gold, silver, and bronze medal.

items.title: Kingdom of Characters - by Jing Tsu
items.summary: Since I’m learning to read and write Chinese, I thought this was going to go more into the history of the characters themselves, but it was about the technological innovations that enabled their adoption and distribution.

items.title: Traits of useful perspectives
items.summary: To list all the beliefs I’ve found useful would fill a whole book.

items.title: Expand your repertoire
items.summary: To change, reach past what comes naturally.

items.title: Diamond in the trash
items.summary: When things aren’t going well, you’re in a bad state of mind.

items.title: Answer great questions
items.summary: Pick something that’s holding you back from what you want to do, be, or feel.

items.title: Who chooses your (next) thoughts?
items.summary: You might say, “I can’t help the way I feel”, as if it’s completely out of your control — as if you have no choice and are unable to feel any other way.

items.title: At the Existentialist Café - by Sarah Bakewell
items.summary: More like a memoir of her re-discovery of existentialist writers. Interesting but because of it, less educational than I wanted.
